explanation: The sentence expresses that, for every instance of DM_i, any TC message generation must be preceded (at some point in the past) by a query to the MPR Selector Set. In LTL, we translate this as: 'Whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then previously there was a query to the MPR Selector Set by the Local Node.' Since LTL works in the forward (future) direction, we can instead specify that a TC message should only occur after a query to the MPR Selector Set. For every TC message generation, it must always be the case that before it, a query to the MPR Selector Set happens. This temporal relation can be formulated as: globally, if a TC message is sent, then previously a query occurred, or more simply, after every query, until the next TC message, no TC message can occur. In terms of basic LTL, this is often expressed by the 'precedence' property: 'A TC message can only be sent if, in the past, a query to the MPR Selector Set occurred.' However, with standard LTL (without past operators), the common way is: in all paths: between two TC messages, a query to the MPR Selector Set happens before the TC message, or that TC message is always preceded by such a query. Using available operators, we can say: Always, if a TC message is sent, then previously, possibly right before, but certainly before, there was a Local Node queried MPR Selector Set. If a TC message is sent, then since the last TC message were sent, the Local Node queried the MPR Selector Set happened. In LTL: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set W !tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)), but since we cannot refer to past in simple LTL, a common pattern is G(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set -> X(F tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)), meaning every query is eventually followed by a TC message, but that does not guarantee the 'preceded by' property. Using only basic operators, we can specify as: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & X(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))) is not accurate. The closest is to use the pattern: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> P(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)), but since we lack past operators, the most faithful basic LTL is to say: it is not possible to have a TC message without, between this and the previous TC, a 'Local Node queried the MPR Selector Set'. A classic sequence constraint is: between two TC messages, a query to the MPR Selector Set always happens. A simple over-approximation is G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)), meaning 'every TC message is eventually (possibly at the same step) followed by a query', but it's not the same as 'immediately before'. Therefore, using the expressiveness of standard LTL, a reasonable encoding is: 'It is always the case that a TC message is not sent unless a query to the MPR Selector Set has already occurred since the last TC message.' Which matches: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set S !tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)), but since the 'since' (S) is a past operator, in basic LTL we approximate as: 'Never does a TC message occur without first a query.' In practice, in standard LTL, we often encode a precedence property as G(!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) -> F tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node. But for the per-event guarantee: the strongest formula in basic LTL is G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & X(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))). Alternatively, if we accept a slight weakening but adheres to available LTL operators, and for simplicity, G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation_dict.natural_language: a TC message generation must be preceded by a query to the MPR Selector Set for every DM_i
Globally, whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, eventually (ideally before, but possibly at the same time) there was a Local Node query to the MPR Selector Set.

explanation: The property states that any TC message generation (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) must be preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set), and that this holds globally for every DM_i. In LTL, 'must be preceded by' is usually specified as: whenever a TC message is sent (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it is required that a query to the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) happened earlier and that nothing in between occurred to invalidate the causality. In LTL, a common pattern is to ensure that whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set must have occurred at some point in the past (or, equivalently, that it is not possible to see a tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node before seeing a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). Since standard LTL does not have a 'past' operator, we can express this with the following equivalent: it is always the case that if a TC message is sent and since the last query no new one has been made, then no TC message is sent until the next query. This can be effectively captured as: globally, any TC message sending is only allowed if there was a query before it and no other TC message in between, or, equivalently, between any two queries, no TC message is sent except potentially immediately after a query. The conventional solution is G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set W tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)), but since we have no weak-until in standard LTL, the closest and safe translation is: globally, if a TC message is sent, then previously a query to MPR Selector Set was made—due to limitations of LTL, the most standard approach is G(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set U tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) or, for a strict requirement, G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (¬tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)). You could also write G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F^-1 local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) if past operators were allowed, but with standard LTL operators only, the most common is G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> P), but since P isn't allowed, G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (¬tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)) is a standard semantical encoding.

explanation: The problem says that for every DM_i, each time a TC message is generated ("tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node"), this must have been preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set ("local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set"). This is a 'precedence' property, which can be expressed as: globally, whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, in the past, there was a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. Standard LTL does not provide past operators, but the forward direction can be captured: globally, it is never the case that tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs before local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. In LTL, the best approximation (future time only) is that whenever a TC message is sent, in the strict past there was a query, or, equivalently, before any tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node can occur, local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set must have occurred. This can be encoded as: it is always the case that if a TC message is sent, then there must have been no such send until a query occurs. So, for each time a TC message can be sent, it must be preceded (possibly immediately) by a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. In forward LTL, this is expressed as: it is never the case that tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs without local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set occurring before. The direct encoding is: Globally, if tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set must have occurred sometime before or at the same time. As LTL can only refer to the future, an accepted encoding is to require that any occurrence of tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is always preceded by (i.e., not occur until) a query. This is formalized as: (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set), and to require that it always holds globally, we can use: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> P), but as past operators are not available, the idiomatic way is: G(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set U tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). Or more simply, every tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is preceded by local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, which is commonly written: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F^-1 local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)), but in LTL with future only: G( (¬tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & X(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) ). However, by expressing that every tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node must be immediately preceded by a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, we can use: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> Ylocal_node_queried_mpr_selector_set), but Y (previous) is not present. Therefore, the standard LTL encoding for future is that tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node cannot happen before local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set: i.e., (not tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. But we need that _every_ tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is preceded by a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, so in total: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set W tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)), but W (weak until) is not present in the allowed operators. The best approximation available is: G(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set || (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)). This means that a TC message can only occur after a query. However, the standard idiomatic way in LTL (future) is: It is always the case that if a TC message is sent, then in the past a query has happened. As a future-only logic, the close approximation is: 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' cannot occur until 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' has occurred, so: (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. This holds initially, but for _every_ occurrence, we want to ensure this, so: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F^past local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). Thus, as an LTL future-only encoding, we use: G(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set || (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation: The property describes that whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it must have been preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). 'Preceded by' in LTL is usually modeled using the implication: whenever the event occurs, it must have been the case that, before this, the other event happened (no possibility that the first holds without the second having occurred since the last such event). In standard LTL, this is modeled using 'whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set happened earlier, and there was no tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node in between'. That is: globally, whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then in the past (strictly before) there was local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, with no tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node between that and now. However, with only future LTL, we use the 'until' operator: globally, it cannot be that a tc_msg is sent before a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. This is usually expressed as: (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) W local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set (where W is weak until), but as W is not allowed, we can use the equivalent: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (not tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)). That is, whenever a TC message is sent, before that, a query of MPR Selector Set must happen and no TC message occurs again before that query. Applying 'for every DM_i' can be covered since the atomic proposition includes DM_i, so the formula holds for each instance. Therefore, the final formula is: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (not tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation: The natural language sentence states that, for every DM_i, the generation of a TC message must be preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set. The phrase 'for every DM_i' can be captured by applying a global condition (G operator). The temporal relation 'must be preceded by' means that whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), there was a point in the past where 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' was true. However, standard LTL can't express 'past' directly, so we use an equivalent future formula: every time a TC message is sent, it is the case that before this can happen, the local node querying the MPR Selector Set must occur without another TC message occurring in-between. In LTL, one common way to express this is to require that it is never the case that a TC message is sent *without* having a query (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) since the last TC message. This can be written as: globally, if a TC message is sent, then in the strict past (or, equivalently, since the previous TC message was sent, there must have been a query). Using the future direction and only allowed operators, we can encode it as: it is always the case that if a TC message is sent, then it has not been possible to send a TC message without first querying the MPR Selector Set; or, equivalently, every TC message is preceded by a (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) that occurs and in-between there is no other TC message. In LTL, this can be encoded as: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation: The requirement states that for every DM_i, the generation of a TC message must be preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set. This means whenever (globally) a TC message is sent (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), there must have been a previous (in the past) query to the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). Since standard LTL does not have past temporal operators, we rewrite the property in terms of the future: It is NEVER the case that a TC message is sent WITHOUT a query to the MPR Selector Set having occurred before. The standard way to encode 'A must be preceded by B' in future LTL is: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)). This expresses that if a TC message is sent, then before that, the system was in a state where local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set was true, and in the interval from the last occurrence of local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set up to the current state, no TC message was sent.

explanation: Instead of generic variables like a, c, b, we now use only variables from the provided set. Therefore, this formula and explanation use meaningful atomic variables. 'Globally if a holds then c is true until b.' Let us choose: (a=hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), (c=msg_forwarded_to_link_set), (b=update_performed_by_neighbor_set). That is: 'Globally, if a HELLO message has been sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then the message is forwarded to Link Set until an update is performed by Neighbor Set.' Translation steps: (1) The antecedent 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' (2) The consequent '(msg_forwarded_to_link_set U update_performed_by_neighbor_set)' (3) The implication: 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (msg_forwarded_to_link_set U update_performed_by_neighbor_set)' (4) Globally applies G over all. Final formula: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (msg_forwarded_to_link_set U update_performed_by_neighbor_set)).

explanation: The statement says: for every DM_i, whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it must have been preceded by a query of the MPR Selector Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set). In LTL, 'must be preceded by' typically means that every occurrence of tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node must have a previous (possibly immediately or at any time in the past) local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. Since standard LTL does not allow referring directly to the past, we can equivalently say: it is never the case that tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs without a prior local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set, i.e., globally, whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node happens, there was a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set at some point before. In pure future LTL, this is often expressed using the weak until operator: in all paths, up to any occurrence of tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node, no such occurrence happens unless preceded by local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. To encode this, the idiomatic way is: '(not tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' should hold before every tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node. So, globally, if tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, it must have been after a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set. The formula is thus: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) & (!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set U tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))). But in standard LTL, the accepted encoding for 'p must be preceded by q' is: !(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node & G(!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)), but to remain within the allowed LTL fragment, another idiomatic way is: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) & (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set))), which is often simplified for practical use to: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set W tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)). Since W (weak until) is not allowed, we use the normal 'U'. Therefore, the translation becomes: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (!tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set)). This ensures that before every tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node, there is a local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set.
